items.question_text: When should an aircraft be informed under deviation advisories?

choices.text: When it is observed in a position and on a track that will obviously cause it to deviate from protected airspace.
Only after it has already left the protected airspace.
Only when the pilot specifically asks whether the aircraft is on course.
Only when the aircraft is below the MEA.

items.explanation: The advisory is issued when the observed position and track will obviously cause a deviation from protected airspace.

items.question_text: If necessary, you must help the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: If necessary, ATC helps the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace.

items.instruction: Is this RNAV width statement correct?
items.question_text: RNAV ATS routes have a width of 8 miles with 4 miles of laterally protected airspace on each side of the route centerline.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note states an 8-mile route width with 4 miles of protected airspace on each side of centerline.

items.instruction: Which limit is the tighter one?
items.question_text: Which route-centerline performance limit applies on an RNAV ATS route?

choices.text: Within 2 miles of the route centerline.
Within 4 miles of the route centerline.
Within 8 miles of the route centerline.
Within 10 miles of the route centerline.

items.explanation: The aircraft system is expected to remain within 2 miles of the route centerline, even though the protected airspace is wider.

items.situation: You observe an RNAV aircraft trending toward the edge of its protected airspace, and its track will obviously take it outside the assigned lateral protection if nothing changes.
items.para_context: Deviation advisories are issued when the observed position and track will obviously cause deviation from protected airspace. If necessary, help the aircraft return. On RNAV ATS routes, aircraft are expected to remain within 2 miles of centerline, and protected airspace extends 4 miles on each side.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft of the developing deviation and help it return to protected airspace if necessary.
Wait until the aircraft is well outside the protected area before saying anything.
Assume the pilot's RNAV system will always self-correct without any advisory.
Terminate radar service because the aircraft is near the route edge.

items.explanation: The controller gives a deviation advisory before the obvious deviation occurs and helps the aircraft return if needed.

items.question_text: When must an observed or reported time over a fix be manually recorded?

choices.text: At least once for each controlled aircraft in the sector when the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.
At most once per shift for all controlled aircraft combined.
Only when the aircraft changes altitude over the fix.
Only for uncontrolled VFR aircraft requesting flight following.

items.explanation: The rule requires at least one manual fix time for each controlled aircraft in the sector when the listed FDP components are not operational.

items.question_text: When the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational, you must manually record the observed or reported time over a fix at least once for each controlled aircraft in your sector.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the stated manual fix posting requirement.

items.question_text: This rule applies to en route operations and to controlled aircraft in the controller's sector of responsibility.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph is specifically scoped to en route operations and controlled aircraft in the sector of responsibility.

items.question_text: If the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are operational, this manual fix posting requirement does not apply.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The manual posting requirement is triggered by those flight progress recording components being not operational.

items.situation: The EAS FDP flight progress recording components have failed in your en route sector, and a controlled aircraft reports over a fix.
items.para_context: In en route operations, when the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational, the controller manually records the observed or reported time over a fix at least once for each controlled aircraft in the sector of responsibility.

choices.text: Manually record the observed or reported time over that fix for the aircraft.
Wait until the aircraft exits the sector, then decide whether any fix time was needed.
Record fix times only for aircraft that request lower altitudes.
Do nothing because manual fix posting is optional if radar remains available.

items.explanation: When the listed FDP components are down, the controller manually records at least one observed or reported time over a fix for each controlled aircraft.

items.question_text: When may a controller request an aircraft to provide an estimate or report over a specific fix?

choices.text: When necessary.
Only before the aircraft is identified by radar.
Only when the aircraft is below 10,000 feet.
Only after radar service has been terminated.

items.explanation: The controller may request a specific fix estimate or report when necessary.

items.question_text: After ATC states "radar contact," the aircraft must discontinue compulsory reporting over reporting points until ATC informs it "radar contact lost" or "radar service terminated."

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Compulsory reporting stops after radar contact and resumes only after radar contact is lost or radar service is terminated.

items.question_text: If ATC informs the aircraft "radar contact lost" or "radar service terminated," the aircraft resumes normal position reporting.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those statements are what cause normal reporting to resume.

items.question_text: Which request is an approved position-report request?

choices.text: Crossing, joining, or departing (airway or route).
Maintain best forward speed to the next fix.
Advise when able for approach.
Line up and wait over the fix.

items.explanation: The paragraph includes position-reporting requests such as crossing, joining, or departing an airway or route.

items.situation: You need a more current position reference from an aircraft to manage the traffic picture, and the aircraft is already receiving radar service.
items.para_context: If necessary, ATC may request an estimate or a report over a specific fix. After radar contact is established, compulsory reporting stops, but the controller may still request a report when needed.

choices.text: Request an estimate or report over the specific fix you need.
Avoid any position-report request because radar contact permanently forbids them.
Terminate radar service first, then request the report.
Wait for the pilot to volunteer a report, even if the information is needed now.

items.explanation: Even after radar contact, the controller may request a specific fix estimate or report when necessary.

items.question_text: What must generally be done when radar service is terminated?

choices.text: Inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated.
Assume the pilot knows radar service ended once the destination airport is in sight.
Terminate the IFR flight plan automatically.
Issue the missed approach instructions immediately.

items.explanation: The general rule is to inform the aircraft when radar service is terminated.

items.instruction: Is this termination phraseology rule correct?
items.question_text: You must use "radar service terminated" when advising an aircraft that radar service has ended, with nonradar routing if required.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The published phraseology is "RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED," with nonradar routing if needed.

items.question_text: If an aircraft cancels its IFR flight plan outside Class B, Class C, TRSA, or other basic-radar-service areas, you may treat radar service as automatically terminated and need not separately advise it.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That cancellation case is one of the listed automatic termination situations, except in the named areas.

items.question_text: Which situation automatically terminates radar service without a separate advisory?

choices.text: An aircraft conducting an instrument, visual, or contact approach has landed or has been instructed to change to advisory frequency.
An arriving aircraft is still five miles from touchdown at a tower-controlled airport with incomplete radar coverage to the runway.
A pilot says they might cancel IFR later in Class C airspace.
A departing aircraft requests a lower altitude after takeoff.

items.explanation: Landing or being instructed to change to advisory frequency during an instrument, visual, or contact approach is one of the listed automatic termination cases.

items.situation: Radar service is ending and no additional routing is needed. Build the advisory.
items.explanation: The correct advisory is "RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED."

items.situation: An arriving aircraft is headed to a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the end of the runway.
items.para_context: At tower-controlled airports where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the runway end, arriving aircraft must be informed when radar service is terminated.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft when radar service is terminated.
Assume the pilot no longer needs the termination advisory because the destination has a tower.
Delay the advisory until after landing.
Terminate radar service silently because the runway is in sight.

items.explanation: In that tower-coverage exception case, the arriving aircraft must be informed when radar service is terminated.

items.question_text: When should an aircraft be informed under deviation advisories?
items.explanation: The advisory is issued when the aircraft's observed position and track will obviously cause a deviation from protected airspace.

choices.text: When the observed position and track will obviously cause deviation from protected airspace.
Only after the aircraft has already exited protected airspace.
Only after the pilot asks whether the route is correct.
Only when the aircraft is below minimum altitude.

items.question_text: If necessary, ATC must help the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace.
items.explanation: The paragraph specifically requires helping the aircraft return when necessary.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What is the total width of an RNAV ATS route described in the note?
items.explanation: The note states an 8-mile total width with 4 miles of protected airspace on each side of centerline.

choices.text: 8 miles.
4 miles.
2 miles.
16 miles.

items.question_text: What route-centerline performance are aircraft on RNAV ATS routes expected to maintain?
items.explanation: The expected performance is remaining within 2 miles of the route centerline.

choices.text: Within 2 miles of the route centerline.
Within 4 miles of the route centerline.
Within 8 miles of the route centerline.
Within 10 miles of the route centerline.

items.question_text: What concern may be indicated by an aircraft approaching the 2-mile RNAV route-centerline limit?
items.explanation: Approaching that limit may indicate a navigation system error or failure.

choices.text: A navigation system error or failure.
A routine increase in route protected width.
Automatic ATC vectoring authority.
Mandatory IFR cancellation.

items.question_text: When must an observed or reported time over a fix be manually recorded?
items.explanation: The time is recorded at least once for each controlled aircraft in the sector when the listed EAS FDP components are not operational.

choices.text: At least once for each controlled aircraft in the sector when the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.
At most once for the sector as a whole.
Only for aircraft changing altitude over the fix.
Only for uncontrolled VFR aircraft.

items.question_text: This manual fix posting requirement applies to en route operations and controlled aircraft in the sector of responsibility.
items.explanation: The paragraph is specifically scoped to en route operations and controlled aircraft in the sector.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What system failure triggers this manual recording requirement?
items.explanation: The trigger is the EAS FDP flight progress recording components being not operational.

choices.text: The EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.
The radar antenna is in standby.
The ATIS recorder is down.
The tower interphone is busy.

items.question_text: How often must the time over a fix be manually recorded for each controlled aircraft in the sector when the rule is active?
items.explanation: The rule requires at least one such entry for each controlled aircraft in the sector.

choices.text: At least once.
At most once per hour.
Exactly twice.
Only if the pilot requests it.

items.question_text: If the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are operational, what is correct about this manual fix posting rule?
items.explanation: The manual posting rule is triggered by those components being not operational, so it does not apply when they are operational.

choices.text: The manual posting requirement does not apply.
You must still manually post every fix time twice.
You must suspend all en route services.
You may record times only for aircraft above FL180.

items.question_text: When may a controller request an aircraft to provide an estimate or report over a specific fix?
items.explanation: The request may be made when necessary.

choices.text: When necessary.
Only before radar identification.
Only below 10,000 feet.
Only after radar service termination.

items.question_text: After ATC states "radar contact," the aircraft discontinues compulsory reporting over compulsory reporting points until ATC states "radar contact lost" or "radar service terminated."
items.explanation: Compulsory reporting stops after radar contact and resumes only after radar contact is lost or radar service is terminated.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When does the aircraft resume normal position reporting?
items.explanation: Normal reporting resumes after ATC states radar contact lost or radar service terminated.

choices.text: When ATC informs it "radar contact lost" or "radar service terminated."
Whenever the aircraft changes altitude.
Only after reaching the destination airport.
Only after canceling IFR.

items.question_text: Which phraseology is published in this paragraph as a position-report request?
items.explanation: The paragraph includes requests such as crossing, joining, or departing an airway or route.

choices.text: Crossing, joining, or departing (airway or route).
Maintain best forward speed.
Resume own navigation direct destination.
Cleared visual approach.

items.question_text: If radar contact exists but you need a more current position reference from the aircraft, what may you do?
items.explanation: The controller may still request an estimate or report over a specific fix when necessary.

choices.text: Request an estimate or report over the specific fix.
Do not request any position information because radar contact permanently prohibits it.
Terminate radar service first, then ask for the report.
Wait for the pilot to volunteer a report whether or not it is needed.

items.question_text: What must generally be done when radar service is terminated?
items.explanation: The general rule is to inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated.
Assume the pilot knows automatically once the airport is in sight.
Terminate IFR automatically.
Issue the missed approach instructions immediately.

items.question_text: What is the standard phraseology for radar service termination?
items.explanation: The published phraseology is "RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED," with nonradar routing if required.

choices.text: Radar service terminated.
Radar contact lost, no further ATC service.
Service terminated, monitor advisory frequency.
Proceed own navigation, no radar available.

items.question_text: If an aircraft cancels IFR outside Class B, Class C, TRSA, or other basic-radar-service areas, does radar service automatically terminate without a separate advisory?
items.explanation: That cancellation case is one of the listed automatic termination situations outside the named areas.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which situation automatically terminates radar service without a separate advisory?
items.explanation: Landing or being instructed to change to advisory frequency during an instrument, visual, or contact approach is one of the listed automatic termination cases.

choices.text: An aircraft conducting an instrument, visual, or contact approach has landed or has been instructed to change to advisory frequency.
An aircraft is still on final at a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the runway end.
A pilot says they might cancel IFR later in Class C airspace.
A departure reports clear of clouds.

items.question_text: At a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the end of the runway, what must be done for arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: In that limited-coverage case, the arriving aircraft must be informed when radar service is terminated.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft when radar service is terminated.
Allow radar service to terminate automatically without saying anything.
Delay the advisory until after touchdown.
Issue only a missed approach advisory instead.
